questions.prompt: What does "go the extra mile" mean?

options.text: To make more effort than is required or expected.
To travel a longer route than necessary.
To finish a task before the deadline.
To win a competition by a large margin.

questions.explanation: "Go the extra mile" means to put in more effort than is required — to exceed what is expected. It has nothing to do with literal travel, beating deadlines, or winning by margins. Those options exploit the word mile to create plausible but wrong literal readings.

questions.prompt: Identify the SIMILE in the following group.

options.text: The news was a bombshell.
Her voice was honey.
The deadline crept up on them like a thief.
The office never sleeps.

questions.explanation: A simile makes an explicit comparison using like or as: "like a thief" is the marker. The other three are metaphors — they describe one thing as another without using like or as (bombshell, honey, never sleeps). Only the third option has the explicit comparative signal that defines a simile.

questions.prompt: Complete: "After the scandal, the CEO tried to ___ by meeting community leaders and donating to local charities."

options.text: hit a brick wall
turn over a new leaf
spill the beans
burn his bridges

questions.explanation: "Turn over a new leaf" means to begin behaving better — fitting the context of repairing a damaged reputation through positive actions. Hitting a brick wall means encountering an obstacle. Spilling the beans means revealing a secret. Burning bridges means irreparably damaging relationships — the opposite of what the CEO is trying to do.

questions.prompt: Find the sentence where figurative language is used CORRECTLY.

options.text: She was over the moon when she heard the bad news.
Sales are selling like hot cakes — they keep stacking up in the warehouse.
He hits the ground running every Monday, arriving at 7 a.m. with a full action plan.
They spilled the bean when they revealed the secret.

questions.explanation: "Hit the ground running" means to begin something with immediate energy and effectiveness — which fits arriving early with a prepared plan. The first option contradicts itself (over the moon = very happy, not appropriate for bad news). The second adds a literal warehouse detail that treats the idiom concretely. The fourth uses the wrong singular form — the fixed idiom is spill the beans, not the bean.

questions.prompt:
Read: "His argument was a house of cards — elegant, intricate, and utterly dependent on assumptions that simply weren't true."
What does the METAPHOR "house of cards" suggest about the argument?

options.text: It was built on a solid, well-researched foundation.
It relied on playing cards as evidence.
It was delivered in an entertaining, game-like manner.
It was visually impressive but dangerously fragile.

questions.explanation: A house of cards is a structure that looks impressive but collapses if any element is disturbed — a perfect metaphor for an argument that appears convincing but rests on false assumptions. The sentence reinforces this with "elegant, intricate" (impressive-looking) followed by "utterly dependent on assumptions that weren't true" (the fragile base). The literal card-playing and entertainment readings are surface distractors.

questions.prompt:
Read: "The merger was the tip of the iceberg. Behind the scenes, the entire sector was burning its bridges with regulators, flooding the market with untested products, and running out of goodwill faster than anyone had anticipated."
Which of the following BEST describes the use of figurative language in this passage?

options.text: Three metaphors and one simile are mixed inconsistently.
All the figurative expressions are used incorrectly, creating confusion.
Multiple idiomatic and figurative expressions are stacked coherently to convey rapid, compounding decline.
The passage uses only one figurative device: the tip of the iceberg.

questions.explanation: The passage layers four figurative expressions — tip of the iceberg (small visible sign of something larger), burning bridges (irreparably damaging relationships), flooding the market (overwhelming with product), running out of goodwill (exhausting trust) — each adding to a picture of accelerating collapse. They are all used correctly in context and build coherently toward the theme of decline. There is no simile and no inconsistency.
